title: Chapter 10: Transfer of Thermal Energy — PYP

questions.question: According to the kinetic theory of matter, thermal energy is transferred from the hot end of a glass rod to the cold end when the molecules from the hot end:
options: emit infrared radiation to the cold end
move from place to place so that they collide with the colder molecules
move to the cold end
vibrate more vigorously and pass on the energy to the neighbouring molecules
questions.explanation:
Directly from 'Let's Review' Q1. Conduction in a solid involves the transfer of vibrational energy between adjacent particles. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking molecules in a solid move from one end to the other.

questions.question: In a vacuum flask, the vacuum between the double walls prevents thermal energy transfer by:
options: conduction only
conduction and convection
convection only
radiation only
questions.explanation:
From 'Let's Review' Q3. Conduction and convection both require a material medium (particles). A vacuum lacks these, so it blocks both processes. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a vacuum also blocks radiation, which can actually pass through empty space.

questions.question: The diagram shows two blocks of the same metal. Block A is at $40^\circ\text{C}$ and Block B is at $20^\circ\text{C}$. They are brought into contact. In which direction does the thermal energy flow?
options: From A to B
From B to A
There is no flow of energy
The direction depends on the mass of the blocks
questions.explanation:
The general principle states energy is transferred from higher temperature to lower temperature. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing temperature with mass or volume.

questions.question: In a hot water tank, the heating element should be placed at the bottom because:
options: conduction cannot take place when the heater is at the top
infrared radiation travels faster in the upward direction
the heated water will rise and this will form convection currents
the heater must be covered by water at all times
questions.explanation:
From 'Let's Review' Q2. Heated water becomes less dense and rises, allowing cold water to sink and be heated, creating a convection current that heats the whole tank. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking conduction is the main way a large tank of water is heated.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies how the rate of thermal energy transfer is affected?
options: Temp. Difference: Increase; Surface Area: Increase; Rate: Increase
Temp. Difference: Decrease; Surface Area: Increase; Rate: Same
Temp. Difference: Increase; Surface Area: Decrease; Rate: Increase
Temp. Difference: Same; Surface Area: Increase; Rate: Decrease
questions.explanation:
The rate of transfer increases with both a larger temperature difference and a larger surface area. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking that decreasing the area would increase the rate because it is 'more concentrated'.

questions.question: According to the kinetic theory of matter, thermal energy is transferred from the hot end of a glass rod to the cold end when the molecules from the hot end:
options: vibrate more vigorously and pass on the energy to the neighbouring molecules
emit infrared radiation to the cold end
move from place to place so that they collide with the colder molecules
move to the cold end
questions.explanation:
In a solid like glass, conduction occurs through the transmission of vibrational energy between adjacent particles. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking molecules in a solid move from one end to the other (Choice D).

questions.question: In a vacuum flask, the vacuum between the double walls prevents thermal energy transfer by:
options: conduction and convection
conduction only
convection only
radiation only
questions.explanation:
Conduction and convection both require a material medium (particles). A vacuum lacks these, so it blocks both processes. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a vacuum also blocks radiation, which can actually pass through empty space.

questions.question: The table shows the thermal conductivity of four materials. Which material would allow thermal energy to pass through it at the slowest rate?nn| Material | Conductivity Rating |n| :--- | :--- |n| P | Best conductor |n| Q | Good conductor |n| R | Poor conductor |n| S | Best insulator |
options: Material S
Material P
Material Q
Material R
questions.explanation:
The 'best insulator' is the 'worst conductor', meaning it has the lowest rate of thermal energy transfer. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting Material P, which allows heat to pass the fastest.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies a conductor and an insulator?
options: Conductor: Copper; Insulator: Polystyrene
Conductor: Steel; Insulator: Aluminum
Conductor: Air; Insulator: Glass
Conductor: Fibreglass; Insulator: Copper
questions.explanation:
Copper is a metal and a good conductor; polystyrene is a plastic that contains air and is a good insulator. 

**Common mistake:** Identifying air as a conductor; it is a gas and a very poor conductor.

questions.question: A student heats water in a test tube. He holds the bottom of the tube with his hand while the water at the top is boiling. He does not feel the heat. What does this experiment demonstrate?
options: Water is a poor conductor of thermal energy
Glass is a perfect thermal insulator
Convection only moves heat downwards
Thermal energy cannot travel through liquids
questions.explanation:
Water is a poor thermal conductor. Because convection moves the hot water upwards, the thermal energy does not conduct efficiently down to the bottom of the tube. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking glass is a 'perfect' insulator; it still conducts, just poorly.

questions.question: A vacuum flask is used to keep a cold drink cool. Which row correctly identifies the function of the vacuum and the silvered surfaces?
options: Vacuum: Stops conduction/convection; Silvering: Reflects external radiation
Vacuum: Stops radiation; Silvering: Stops conduction
Vacuum: Stops convection only; Silvering: Stops all heat transfer
Vacuum: Reflects radiation; Silvering: Stops convection
questions.explanation:
The vacuum removes particles needed for conduction and convection. The silvering reflects infrared radiation from the surroundings away from the cold drink. 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the roles of the vacuum and the silvered surfaces.

questions.question: Which set of conditions for a house in a cold climate would result in the lowest rate of thermal energy loss?
options: Double-glazed windows and fibreglass in the roof
Large single-pane windows and a black roof
Open ventilation and metal external walls
Thin wooden walls and no ceiling insulation
questions.explanation:
Double-glazing and fibreglass both utilize trapped air as an insulator to significantly reduce conduction and convection losses. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a black roof is better for keeping heat 'in'; it would actually emit more radiation.

questions.question: The table shows the thermal conductivity of four materials. Based on this, which material should be used for the base of a frying pan and which for the handle?nn| Material | Conductivity |n| :--- | :--- |n| P | Very High |n| Q | Medium |n| R | Low |n| S | Very Low |
options: Base: P; Handle: S
Base: S; Handle: P
Base: Q; Handle: R
Base: P; Handle: Q
questions.explanation:
The base needs to conduct heat quickly to the food (P), while the handle needs to be an insulator to protect the user (S). 

**Common mistake:** Choosing 'Very High' (P) for both, which would result in a burnt hand.

questions.question: A graph is plotted showing the temperature of hot water in two identical flasks over time. Flask A is silvered and Flask B is black. Which description of the graph is correct?
options: The line for Flask B has a steeper negative gradient than Flask A
The line for Flask A has a steeper negative gradient than Flask B
Both flasks will show identical horizontal lines
The lines will have a positive gradient as the water warms up
questions.explanation:
The black flask (B) is a better emitter of radiation, so it loses energy faster. This results in a steeper temperature drop (gradient) on the graph. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the silver flask cools faster because it 'reflects' heat outwards.

questions.question: Why does wearing several layers of thin clothing keep a person warmer than one single layer of thick clothing?
options: Thin layers trap layers of still air which are good insulators
Thin layers are better at absorbing infrared radiation from the skin
The friction between the multiple layers creates extra thermal energy
Multiple layers increase the total mass and thus the heat capacity
questions.explanation:
The air trapped between the thin layers acts as an excellent insulator, preventing heat loss by conduction and convection. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the material itself provides the heat rather than just preventing its loss.

questions.question: In a hot water tank, the heating element should be placed at the bottom because:
options: the heated water will rise and this will form convection currents
infrared radiation travels faster in the upward direction
conduction cannot take place when the heater is at the top
the heater must be covered by water at all times
questions.explanation: Directly from the 'Let's Review' section (Q2). Heated water is less dense and rises, allowing the whole tank to be heated via a current. **Common mistake:** Thinking it's purely about keeping the heater submerged.

questions.question: A student observes smoke in a box with two chimneys and a candle under one of them. What determines the direction of the air flow?
options: The rising of hot, less dense air above the candle
The suction caused by the candle consuming oxygen
The conduction of heat through the metal box
The magnetic effect of the current in the air
questions.explanation: The candle heats the air, which rises up its chimney. This creates a low-pressure area that draws cold air (and smoke) down the other chimney. **Common mistake:** Thinking the candle 'sucks' the air in like a vacuum cleaner.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies properties of convection?nn| | Medium Required | Method of Energy Transfer |n| :--- | :--- | :--- |n| A | Yes | Bulk movement of particles |n| B | No | Bulk movement of particles |n| C | Yes | Vibrations of particles |n| D | No | Infrared waves |
options: Row A
Row B
Row C
Row D
questions.explanation: Convection requires a medium (fluid) and involves the bulk movement of the particles. **Common mistake:** Selecting Row B; only radiation can occur without a medium.

questions.question: A graph of density against temperature for a typical gas at constant pressure would show:
options: Density decreasing as temperature increases
Density increasing as temperature increases
Density remaining constant as temperature increases
Density increasing then decreasing
questions.explanation: As temperature increases, the gas expands (volume increases). Since mass is constant, density ($m/V$) must decrease. **Common mistake:** Thinking density increases because particles move 'faster'.

questions.question: Why is trapped, still air considered an excellent thermal insulator in building materials?
options: It is a poor conductor and cannot form convection currents
It reflects all thermal radiation away from the building
It has a very high specific heat capacity
It is denser than moving air, preventing heat flow
questions.explanation: As mentioned in the 'Enrichment Think' box, still air is a poor conductor, and 'trapping' it prevents convection currents from transferring energy. **Common mistake:** Thinking air 'reflects' heat.

questions.question: Thermal energy from the Sun reaches the Earth through space. Which statement explains why this transfer cannot be by conduction or convection?
options: There are no particles in the vacuum of space.
The Sun is at a higher temperature than the Earth.
Infrared radiation travels faster than the speed of sound.
The Sun's gravitational field is too strong for convection.
questions.explanation:
Conduction and convection require a material medium (particles). Space is a vacuum, so only radiation can transfer energy. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking radiation is just 'stronger' than the other methods.

questions.question: The hot pipes at the back of a refrigerator are painted black. What is the physical reason for this?
options: Black surfaces are better emitters of radiation than white surfaces.
Black surfaces are better absorbers of radiation from the room.
Black paint is a better conductor of heat than white paint.
Black surfaces prevent the pipes from expanding too much.
questions.explanation:
Dull black surfaces are efficient emitters, which helps the refrigerator dissipate heat to the surroundings more effectively. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the black color is meant to 'absorb' heat from inside the pipes.

questions.question: A student plots a graph of the temperature of water in two identical cans, one black and one silver, as they cool. Which description of the graph is correct?
options: The curve for the black can is steeper than for the silver can.
The curve for the silver can is steeper than for the black can.
Both cans will show identical straight lines.
The curves will cross each other halfway through the cooling process.
questions.explanation:
The black can emits radiation more rapidly, meaning its temperature drops faster, resulting in a steeper gradient on the cooling curve. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the silver can's curve is steeper because it is 'shinier'.

questions.question: Which row in the table correctly identifies the properties of a dull black surface compared to a shiny white surface?
options: Absorption: Better; Emission: Better; Reflection: Worse
Absorption: Worse; Emission: Better; Reflection: Better
Absorption: Better; Emission: Worse; Reflection: Worse
Absorption: Worse; Emission: Worse; Reflection: Better
questions.explanation:
Dull black surfaces are the best absorbers and emitters but the worst reflectors. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a 'good absorber' must be a 'poor emitter'.

questions.question: Which feature of a vacuum flask is designed to reduce thermal energy transfer by radiation?
options: Silvered surfaces
The vacuum space
Plastic stopper
Cork supports
questions.explanation:
Silvered surfaces reflect infrared radiation, whereas the vacuum blocks conduction and convection. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting 'vacuum' for radiation; radiation can actually pass through a vacuum.
